questions.question: When you call a generic Rust function with two different concrete types, what happens at compile time?
options: Monomorphization: the compiler generates a separate, specialized copy of the function for each concrete type combination it sees. Zero runtime cost; some binary-size and compile-time cost.
Boxing: the value is wrapped in an object
Nothing — there's one function for all types
questions.explanation: Rust generics are monomorphized — every distinct (T, U, ...) used produces a fresh function in the binary. This is the same model as C++ templates. The trade-off: blazing-fast runtime (compiler can inline freely) at the cost of bigger binaries. Java generics, by contrast, use type erasure — one bytecode at runtime.

questions.question: What's the Rust equivalent of a Java `interface`?
options: trait — declares method signatures that types can implement; functions can generically operate over any T that implements the trait
questions.explanation: Trait = interface = type class (Haskell) = concept (C++20). They all mean 'a set of methods this type promises to provide.' Rust traits are closer to Haskell type classes — you can implement them for types you don't own, and dispatch is static unless you opt into `dyn`.

questions.question: When would you choose `Box<dyn Trait>` over a generic `<T: Trait>`?
options: When you need a heterogeneous collection (different types in the same Vec), or a stable ABI that doesn't change with each generic instantiation
questions.explanation: Generics (static dispatch) win on performance (~free, inlinable) but produce a specialized copy per type — useless for heterogeneous data. Trait objects (dyn dispatch) box up the value behind a vtable; you pay a runtime indirection (~2 cycles per call, no inlining) but get one binary form. Use generics by default; switch to dyn when you genuinely need erasure.

questions.question: What does the bound `fn max<T: PartialOrd>(a: T, b: T) -> T` ensure?
options: T must implement the `PartialOrd` trait, so `a < b` and `a > b` are defined in the body; the compiler refuses to compile if T doesn't satisfy the bound.
T must be small enough to fit in a register
questions.explanation: Trait bounds are CONTRAINTS the compiler enforces. The body of `max` uses `>`, which is sugar for `PartialOrd::gt`. The bound `T: PartialOrd` tells the compiler this is OK; without it the function wouldn't compile. C++ concepts (C++20) do the same; Haskell uses `=>`.

questions.question: Why doesn't Rust have inheritance like Java or C++?
options: Composition over inheritance: traits + generics handle every pattern inheritance offers (polymorphism, code reuse, type hierarchies) without the fragility of deep class trees. Rust deliberately encourages a flatter design.
It's coming in the next version
questions.explanation: The Rust team chose to skip inheritance entirely. Patterns it would have handled (polymorphism → traits; code reuse → trait default methods + composition; covariance → generic bounds) are all expressible. The fragility of multi-level inheritance hierarchies is well-documented (Yo-yo problem, fragile base class) and Rust avoids it by design.

questions.question: How is Rust's `impl std::ops::Add for Vec2` analogous to Python's `__add__` or C++'s `operator+`?
options: All three implement operator overloading: defining what `+` means for a custom type. Rust does it via a trait; Python via a dunder method; C++ via a member or free `operator+` function. Same idea, different syntax.
questions.explanation: Operator overloading is the practical face of ad-hoc polymorphism. Rust implements `+` by impl'ing `std::ops::Add`. C++ defines `operator+` as a member or free function. Python defines `__add__`. The compiler/interpreter rewrites `a + b` to call your code. Used everywhere: matrix math, decimal types, time arithmetic, even string concatenation.
